labels.dispersionHint: Random velocity change applied to each fragment (1 sigma). In-track stretches the cloud along the orbit; cross-track fans it across the orbital plane; radial raises and lowers it. Typical events: explosions about 10 to 100 m/s, collisions about 100 to 300 m/s.

toasts.fragmentsReentered: {count} fragments reentered and were not created.

help.overview: The Breakup menu simulates a satellite breaking apart at its current position. It spawns debris pieces from the selected satellite's exact location, each leaving with a small random velocity change (delta-V), so you can watch the debris cloud form and spread over time. Because it works from the satellite's state vector, it supports any orbital regime.
help.imgAlt: The Breakup menu with delta-V dispersion and piece-count controls
help.imgCaption: Breakup parameters. Larger delta-V values spread the simulated debris cloud wider.
help.parameters:
• Radial: velocity spread toward and away from Earth (m/s), which raises and lowers the fragments.
  • In-Track: velocity spread along the direction of motion (m/s), which stretches the cloud along the orbit.
  • Cross-Track: velocity spread out of the orbital plane (m/s), which fans the cloud across the plane.
  • Pieces: how many debris fragments to create (5 to 1000).
  • Initial Satellite Number: the first analyst catalog number to assign to the pieces (defaults to 90000).
The values are treated as a one-sigma spread per fragment. The defaults (about 40 m/s per axis) model an explosion-class event; real explosion fragments spread by roughly 10 to 100 m/s, while catastrophic collisions reach roughly 100 to 300 m/s (small fragments can exceed 1 km/s). Keep the three axes equal for a physically realistic, spherically symmetric event; the cloud naturally stretches along the orbit over time.
help.howToUse:
1. Select any satellite. The icon stays disabled until a satellite is selected.
  2. Open the Breakup menu and adjust the delta-V dispersion and piece count.
  3. Click Create Breakup. The pieces are generated at the satellite's current position and the search bar fills with the new debris so you can see the whole cloud.
  4. Speed up time to watch the cloud disperse along the orbit.
help.tip1: Works in any orbital regime, including LEO, MEO, GEO, and highly elliptical (HEO) orbits, because every fragment is propagated from the parent's state vector at the breakup point.
help.tip2: The breakup is simulated and in-memory only. Use Clear Breakup to remove the debris, or reload the page. The parent satellite itself is never modified.
help.tip3: All pieces are created as debris-type objects, so debris-based color schemes and filters apply to them immediately.

errorMsgs.InvalidStartNum: Invalid satellite starting number! Defaulting to 90000!
errorMsgs.InvalidSlotRange: Initial satellite number out of range. Use {start} to {end}.
